article.title: Waymo Robotaxis Have Required First Responder Intervention in at Least Six Emergencies, TechCrunch Investigation Finds
article.summary: Police and firefighters have repeatedly been called to drive stalled Waymo vehicles out of danger zones, raising questions about autonomous vehicle preparedness as California's AB 1777 emergency response law takes effect in July.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

First responders across California have been forced to physically take control of Waymo robotaxis and drive them out of harm's way during emergencies on at least six occasions, according to an [investigation published by TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/25/waymo-robotaxi-roadside-assistance-emergency-first-responders/). The incidents include situations where police officers were diverted from active crime scenes and wildfire evacuations to move stalled autonomous vehicles, raising pointed questions about whether the robotaxi industry is prepared for real-world emergency scenarios.

The findings arrive as California's AB 1777, which mandates dedicated emergency hotlines and two-minute geofencing compliance for autonomous vehicle operators, is set to take effect on July 1, 2026.

## What We Know

TechCrunch identified at least six instances in which emergency personnel had to take physical control of Waymo vehicles during crisis situations. In one case, a police officer who was responding to a mass shooting was first required to move a stalled Waymo robotaxi that was blocking the route, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/25/waymo-robotaxi-roadside-assistance-emergency-first-responders/).

Another incident occurred last August near Redwood City, California, when a grass fire swept across roughly 10 acres on both sides of Interstate 280. California Highway Patrol officers directed drivers to turn around and exit the highway against the flow of traffic. A Waymo robotaxi attempted to bypass stopped traffic by driving along the shoulder but was unable to execute a U-turn and stopped completely. Waymo's remote assistance team could not resolve the situation, and the company called 911. A remote operator told a 911 dispatcher: "Highway patrol turned everyone around, but unfortunately our car is not able to turn around," as [reported by TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/25/waymo-robotaxi-roadside-assistance-emergency-first-responders/). Roughly 30 minutes later, a CHP officer got behind the wheel and drove the robotaxi to a nearby park-and-ride lot.

The pattern of first responder dependency became especially visible during a major power outage in San Francisco on December 20, 2025. A PG&E substation fire knocked out electricity for roughly one-third of the city, causing widespread disruption to Waymo's fleet as vehicles struggled to navigate intersections with non-functioning traffic signals, as [reported by CNBC](https://www.cnbc.com/2025/12/21/waymo-robotaxi-san-francisco-blackout.html). Data presented at a subsequent Board of Supervisors hearing revealed the fleet stopped 1,593 times for two minutes or more during the outage, with 64 Jaguar I-PACE vehicles requiring staff or tow truck assistance and first responders physically intervening with two vehicles, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/25/waymo-robotaxi-roadside-assistance-emergency-first-responders/). A 911 dispatcher reportedly waited 53 minutes on hold with Waymo's first responder hotline during the outage.

At a testy March 2 Board of Supervisors hearing about the blackout response, Waymo's public policy manager Michael Magee acknowledged: "We did not meet our standards during the outage." District supervisor Alan Wong summarized the frustration shared by multiple officials: "Our first responders should not be AAA," according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/25/waymo-robotaxi-roadside-assistance-emergency-first-responders/).

Waymo currently employs approximately 70 remote operators managing around 3,000 vehicles nationwide. These operators provide "confirmation checks" but cannot remotely drive the vehicles. The company also maintains a separate roadside assistance team, though TechCrunch noted that Waymo's representatives never mentioned these workers during the March 2 hearing, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/29/techcrunch-mobility-when-a-robotaxi-has-to-call-911/).

## What We Don't Know

The full scope of first responder interventions remains unclear. TechCrunch identified six incidents, but acknowledged these were the cases it was able to document. The actual number may be higher, particularly across Waymo's expanding operations in San Francisco, Los Angeles, Phoenix, and Austin.

It is also uncertain how Waymo's remote assistance infrastructure compares to that of competitors such as Zoox and Cruise. Whether the 70-operator-to-3,000-vehicle ratio is industry standard or an outlier has not been publicly benchmarked.

The financial burden placed on municipalities by these interventions has not been quantified. San Francisco's Department of Emergency Management executive director Mary Ellen Carroll stated that first responders are "becoming a default roadside assistance for these vehicles," but no city has yet calculated the cost of diverted public safety resources.

## Analysis

California's AB 1777 law, which takes effect July 1, 2026, will require autonomous vehicle operators to maintain dedicated emergency response telephone lines during all hours of operation and to equip vehicles with two-way voice communication devices so that first responders near a stalled vehicle can speak directly with a remote operator. The law also authorizes emergency officials to issue geofencing messages that require operators to clear their fleets from a designated area within two minutes.

The 53-minute hold time recorded during the December blackout illustrates the gap between current performance and the legal standard that AB 1777 will impose. Waymo has announced software updates and improved escalation protocols but has declined to share implementation details or commit to specific staffing increases.

The incidents documented by TechCrunch represent a category of risk that has received less attention than collisions: the burden autonomous vehicles place on emergency services when they encounter scenarios outside their operational design domain. As robotaxi fleets scale into dozens of cities, the question of who bears responsibility when a self-driving car cannot drive itself is becoming increasingly difficult to defer.
